problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem A (continuous): predict where Scottish herring and mackerel will migrate over the next 50 years as ocean temperatures rise, determine best/worst/most-likely time until the fish are too far for small (no on-board refrigeration) fishing companies to reach, recommend operational changes, and analyze the effect of fish crossing into another country's territorial waters. This O-award paper (control #2001334) chains three models: a sea-surface-temperature time-series forecaster, a temperature-gradient-driven fish migration simulator, and a fishing-company profit/bankruptcy evaluator, all stitched together by a 10,000-run Monte Carlo (bootstrap) over forecast uncertainty.
models_used: Model I — Sea-surface-temperature forecaster: per-pixel ARIMA(p,d,0) over 690 grid cells of monthly SST (1960-2019, April-July means). Order selected as ARIMA(1,1,0) by 6-fold cross-validation on RMSE; parameters estimated by closed-form OLS on the differenced lag matrix (alpha_hat = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y) rather than a black-box solver.

Bootstrap / Monte Carlo uncertainty propagation: residual variance from ARIMA used to draw multivariate-normal innovations (mvnrnd over the covariance of differenced residuals), generating 10,000 stochastic 50-year temperature trajectories that feed every downstream model.

Model II — Fish migration model split into kinematics + kinetics: position update (x_t,y_t)=(x_{t-1},y_{t-1})+v dt driven by a temperature-gradient velocity law v = sign(Δu) · ∇u/(||∇u||+β) · v_max. This is a logistic-saturation-style advection: fish move down-temperature toward their preferred band [u_min=2°C, u_max=6°C], capped at v_max≈188.6 km/yr, with β as a small smoothing/scaling constant. Gradient ∇u approximated by finite differences; continuous field via linear interpolation of the discrete ARIMA forecast.

Model III — Fishing-company earnings model: profit I = ρ(x,y)·θ·e^{-λd(x,y)} - a·d(x,y) - C_0, where density ρ gives catch, the exponential e^{-λd} models freshness/price decay with distance d to port (no refrigeration), and cost is fixed C_0 plus per-distance a. λ calibrated from the half-life assumption e^{-λL̂}=0.5 ⇒ λ=ln2/L̂; θ back-solved from FAO mean financial data.

Monte Carlo bankruptcy estimator: 10,000 × 50-year nested simulation produces an empirical distribution of the year a company can no longer fish, yielding best/worst/most-likely cases (2070 / 2030 / 2039) as probabilities.

Geographic coordinate handling: GRS80 geodesic distances on the lat/lon quadrilateral fit to a projection transform so that Euclidean distance ≈ geodesic distance for the velocity and cost calculations.

Sensitivity analysis via first-order finite differences of survival rate η w.r.t. social profit rate r and navigation radius L; robustness via pairwise RMSE between final distributions from different random initial fish distributions, shown stable on a Q-Q plot.
key_techniques: Stationarity-first model justification: plotted 60-year SST series, argued 'no obvious trend,' verified stationarity, and used that to justify ARIMA over a deterministic-trend model — model choice is earned, not asserted.
Hand-rolled ARIMA with OLS closed form instead of a toolbox function, making the math fully transparent and defensible to judges (full MATLAB in appendix).
6-fold cross-validation on RMSE to pick (p,d) among {(1,1),(1,2),(2,1),(2,2)} — turns model-order selection into a reproducible, data-driven decision with a box-plot exhibit.

Bootstrap to propagate forecast uncertainty end-to-end: instead of a single point forecast, 10,000 sampled trajectories convert every answer into a probability (e.g., 8.25% bankrupt-by-2039), directly answering the prompt's best/worst/most-likely framing.

Physically-motivated velocity law: bounded the migration speed with limits (Δu→∞ ⇒ v→v_max, Δu→0 ⇒ v→0) and chose a logistic-style functional form to satisfy those limits, then calibrated β and v_max from data.

Calibration by economic identities rather than guessing: λ from a freshness half-life assumption, θ from FAO mean cost/profit ratios — every parameter in Table 3/Table 4 is traceable to a source or an assumption.

Strategy evaluation by re-running the same simulator under counterfactuals (move port to Iceland; install refrigeration when territorial-sea law blocks the move), reporting survival-rate deltas (100% vs 62.68%) — directly answers Problems 3 and 4 with the existing machinery.
why_it_won: "It fully and explicitly answers all four sub-questions in the prompt's own language — 'most likely location,' and 'best/worst/most-likely elapsed time' — and converts each into a quantified probability rather than a single number, which is exactly what the problem's best/worst/most-likely phrasing invites. The three-model pipeline is cleanly decomposed (temperature → migration → economics) yet tightly coupled through one bootstrap loop, so judges see both modularity and integration. Crucially, the connective tissue is rigorous: model order chosen by cross-validation, parameters estimated by transparent OLS with the closed form shown, uncertainty propagated by 10,000-run Monte Carlo, and the whole thing validated by BOTH a sensitivity analysis (survival vs r and L, trends matching intuition) AND a robustness check (final distribution invariant to random initial conditions, shown on a Q-Q plot). The economic model is grounded in real FAO financial data and a clever freshness-decay term that ties the no-refrigeration constraint directly into the objective. Problems 3 and 4 are answered by re-running the same engine under counterfactual strategies, demonstrating the model is actually decision-useful. Reproducibility is high: full MATLAB for both ARIMA fitting and the bootstrap migration sim is in the appendix."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet leads with all three model names and immediately gives the headline probabilistic answers (worst 2030 @0.02%, most-likely 2039 @8.25%, best no-bankruptcy-in-50yr @5.27%) — judges get the punchline in paragraph one. The Model Overview mermaid-style flowchart (Figure 2) makes the temperature→gradient→speed→position→profit chain legible at a glance. Each of the four General Assumptions carries an explicit Justification (law of large numbers for predictable group migration, depth-change negligible vs horizontal range, ceteris-paribus on macro factors), so simplifications are defended not hidden. Notation table, data-source table (APDRC/NOAA/Sea Around Us/FAO), and an honest data-cleaning subsection (April-July means, linear interpolation of gaps, columns with ≥4 missing marked unreachable) establish credibility early. The Test-the-Model section separates sensitivity (Fig 15) from robustness (Q-Q plot, Fig 16). The paper closes with a one-page plain-language 'Watch for fish migration' letter to fishermen — concrete, actionable advice (move ports / upgrade vessels / expand navigation radius / low-carbon travel) that satisfies the communication deliverable. Appendices include tooling (LaTeX, MATLAB R2019a) and complete code."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Per-cell independent ARIMA(1,1,0) treats 690 grid points as independent series — despite calling it 'vector autoregression' in the prose, the implemented model has no spatial coupling, so it cannot exploit spatial temperature correlation and the 'VAR' framing is inaccurate.

Justifying ARIMA on 'no obvious trend' partly contradicts the entire premise (warming oceans); a d=1 difference plus AR(1) can drift, but the model has no explicit climate-forcing/warming trend term, so 50-year extrapolation rests on the differenced AR dynamics alone and is weakly grounded for a multi-decade horizon.

Several equation typos/inconsistencies: Eq (11) writes ∂u/∂x and ∂u/∂y both divided by dt (should be dx, dy); profit is called both P(x,y) and I(x,y) (Eqs 14-15 reuse I for income and profit); Assumption 4's justification contains a garbled sentence ('believed that they are fake'). These are sloppiness that a stricter judge would penalize.

The migration velocity law v=sign(Δu)·∇u/(||∇u||+β)·v_max is asserted as 'logistic-style' but is really a normalized-gradient advection with a hard temperature on/off (code sets delta_u=-1 if T>6, +1 if T<2, else 0) — it is heuristic, not derived from fish behavioral ecology, and β=1.05e-5 is essentially negligible so speed is near-constant v_max wherever a gradient exists.
Key economic parameters are loosely sourced: C_0=1616$, a=3.01$/km, θ=9440.73 from 'FAO mean data' without per-company variance, yet results are reported to false precision (8.25%, 62.68%, 5.27%).

Bankruptcy probabilities are internally inconsistent in places (worst-case 2030 quoted at 0.02% probability while described as the worst case), and the histogram/relative-frequency exhibits are hard to reconcile with the stated headline numbers.

Robustness test only varies the initial fish distribution; it does not stress-test the ARIMA forecast, the velocity-law functional form, or the economic parameters — the most uncertain pieces are left unperturbed.
reusable_patterns: Three-stage 'physics → behavior → economics' pipeline coupled by one Monte Carlo loop: forecast the driver (temperature), simulate the agent response (migration), score the human/economic outcome (profit) — a transferable template for any climate/resource/agent problem.

Bootstrap-everything: fit a stochastic forecaster, draw N (here 10,000) trajectories from its residual distribution, push each through the full downstream model, and report answers as empirical probability distributions (best/worst/most-likely). This directly satisfies prompts that ask for scenario ranges.
Earn your model choice: plot the series, test stationarity, and use k-fold CV on RMSE to select model order, presenting the selection as a box-plot exhibit rather than asserting the model.
Closed-form OLS for AR fitting shown explicitly (alpha=(X^T X)^{-1}X^T Y) plus appendix code — maximizes transparency/reproducibility versus a toolbox call.

Gradient-descent-on-a-field agent motion: position update + a bounded velocity = f(∇field) with limit conditions (v→0 and v→v_max) used to choose the functional form, then data-calibrated. Reusable for migration, diffusion, or flow problems.
Calibrate parameters via interpretable identities/assumptions (half-life ⇒ λ=ln2/L̂; back-solve a scale factor from observed means) so every constant is traceable to a source or a stated assumption.
Answer 'what should they do?' sub-questions by re-running the same simulator under counterfactual policies and reporting survival-rate deltas — no new model needed.
Split validation into sensitivity (∂outcome/∂parameter by finite difference, check trend plausibility) and robustness (invariance of output to randomized inputs, shown via Q-Q plot).
Geodesic-to-planar projection fit so Euclidean distance approximates true distance, letting you do all downstream math in flat coordinates.
Close with a plain-language stakeholder letter that restates the probabilistic findings as concrete actions — satisfies the communication deliverable MCM judges weight.
